title: Case on Acting President’s Failure to Appoint Justice Elected by National Assembly
text:
1Case on Acting President’s Failure to Appoint Justice Elected by National Assembly
[2025Hun-Ra1, February 27, 2025]
* First Draft
Claimant, the National Assembly, elected three Constitutional Court Justices by resolution 
at its plenary session on December 26, 2024. Respondent, the Minister of Economy and 
Finance serving as Acting President, appointed two of them as Justices as of January 1, 2025, 
but did not appoint the remaining person as a Justice. The Court held that Respondent’s 
failure to appoint the remaining person as a Justice constituted an infringement of Claimant’s 
constitutionally granted authority to form the Court through the election of Justices. 
Background of the Case
1. The three Justices elected by the National Assembly (Claimant) and appointed by the 
President on October 18, 2018 retired upon the expiration of their terms on October 17, 2024, 
and the National Assembly was required to elect their successors. On December 9, 2024, 
among the parliamentary groups in the National Assembly, the parliamentary group of the 
Democratic Party of Korea recommended two candidates and the People Power Party 
recommended one candidate for Justice. After procedures such as the introduction of the 
proposal for the election of Justices and the holding of confirmation hearings, Claimant, on 
December 26, 2024, adopted in a plenary session a resolution to elect the three candidates as 
Justices.
2. On December 14, 2024, the National Assembly passed a resolution to impeach the 
President, and on December 27, 2024, the Prime Minister. Consequently, the exercise of 
powers by both the President and the Prime Minister was suspended, and the Minister of 
Economy and Finance (Respondent) assumed the powers of the President. Serving as Acting 
President, Respondent, on January 1, 2025, appointed two of the persons elected as Justices 
but did not appoint the remaining person, Ma Eunhyeok, as a Justice. 
3. On January 3, 2025, Claimant filed the instant competence dispute case, seeking both a 
decision confirming that Respondent’s failure to appoint Ma Eunhyeok as a Justice infringed 
Claimant’s authority to elect Justices and to form the Court, and a decision confirming Ma 
2Eunhyeok’s status as a Justice or ordering Respondent to appoint him immediately as a 
Justice. 
4. On February 14, 2025, Claimant adopted in a plenary session the “Resolution Calling 
for the Appointment of Constitutional Court Justice Ma Eunhyeok (Substitute Resolution),” 
which stated, inter alia, that “the National Assembly of the Republic of Korea supports the 
competence dispute case (2025Hun-Ra1) seeking to give effect to the resolution on the 
proposal for the election of Constitutional Court Justice Ma Eunhyeok, and reaffirms that the 
filing and litigation acts of the National Assembly are valid and lawful acts.”
Subject Matter of Review
The subject matter of review in this case is ⅰ) whether Respondent’s failure, as Acting 
President, to appoint Ma Eunhyeok, whom Claimant elected as a Justice on December 26, 
2024 (hereinafter referred to as the “failure to appoint at issue”), infringed Claimant’s 
constitutional or statutory authority (hereinafter referred to as the “claim for confirmation of 
infringement of authority”); and ⅱ) whether the Court may render a decision confirming that 
Ma Eunhyeok, whom Claimant elected as a Justice on December 26, 2024, holds the status of 
Justice or ordering Respondent to appoint him immediately as a Justice (“claim for 
confirmation of status or order for appointment”).
Summary of the Decision
1. Assessment of the claim for confirmation of status or order for appointment 
The claim for confirmation of status or order for appointment may be understood as 
requesting the Court to render a decision conferring upon Ma Eunhyeok the legal status of 
Justice. However, where a failure to act by one State organ infringes the authority of another, 
there is no basis in the Constitution or the Constitutional Court Act for the Court to go 
beyond confirming such infringement of authority and render a decision establishing a legal 
relationship. As this claim seeks a form of decision not envisaged under Article 66, Sections 
(1) and (2) of the Constitutional Court Act, it is nonjusticiable.
32. Assessment of the claim for confirmation of infringement of authority
A. The portion of the instant claim seeking confirmation of infringement of authority is 
grounded in Claimant’s election of Justices through the plenary session resolution of 
December 26, 2024. By that resolution, Claimant expressed its will regarding the exercise of 
its constitutional authority to elect Justices. After the Prime Minister, serving as Acting 
President, indicated that he would appoint those elected by Claimant as Justices only upon 
confirmation of an agreement between the ruling and opposition parties, Claimant adopted on 
December 27, 2024 a resolution of impeachment against the Prime Minister. This can be 
regarded as confirmation, through the plenary session resolution, that Claimant’s authority of 
election was infringed by the Prime Minister serving as Acting President. In light of these 
circumstances, and considering, inter alia, the significant constitutional necessity of 
clarifying and restoring the constitutional order governing the composition of the Court, the 
portion of the instant claim brought by the Speaker of the National Assembly on behalf of 
Claimant must be regarded as duly filed, even though no resolution on an independent agenda 
item addressing whether to file that portion of the claim was adopted in the plenary session.
B. Article 111, Sections (2) and (3) of the Constitution provide that nine Justices of the 
Constitutional Court shall be appointed by the President, three of whom shall be those elected 
by Claimant and three of whom shall be those nominated by the Chief Justice of the Supreme 
Court. This arrangement is intended to ensure checks and balances among the legislative, 
executive, and judicial branches by allowing each to participate equally in the composition of 
the Court, and to secure that the Court —entrusted with the mission of safeguarding the 
Constitution and protecting the fundamental rights of the people —performs its constitutional 
adjudicatory functions from a neutral position. In particular, entrusting Claimant, the 
representative body of the people, with involvement in the composition of the Court has 
fundamental significance in enhancing democratic legitimacy. In light of the text or purpose 
of Article 111, Section (3) of the Constitution, the authority to elect Justices conferred upon 
Claimant in connection with appointments to the Court is independent and substantive. As 
such, the President has no discretion to arbitrarily refuse to appoint, to selectively appoint, or 
otherwise to substantively review persons elected by Claimant in order to decide whether to 
4appoint them as Justices. The President may withhold appointment and request a new election 
if a person elected as a Justice does not meet the qualifications prescribed by Article 111, 
Section (2) of the Constitution and Article 5 of the Constitutional Court Act, or if defects 
exist in the election process that violate the Constitution —which is founded on the principle 
of parliamentary democracy —or statutes such as the National Assembly Act.
The President’s exercise of the authority to appoint Justices is not only his prerogative but 
also a constitutional duty to ensure that the Court, as a constitutional organ, is formed so that 
it may carry out its constitutional adjudicatory functions from a neutral position for the 
safeguarding of the Constitution and the protection of the fundamental rights of the people. 
Therefore, the President ––or the Prime Minister or other State Council member serving as 
Acting President ––bears a constitutional duty to appoint as a Justice any person elected by 
Claimant, provided that such person satisfies the qualifications prescribed by the Constitution 
and the Constitutional Court Act and that no defects exist in the election process that are 
contrary to the Constitution, which is founded on the principle of parliamentary democracy, 
or to statutes such as the National Assembly Act.
C. The three individuals elected as Justices by Claimant on December 26, 2024 meet the 
qualifications set forth above, and no defects can be regarded as existing in the election 
process as described above. Thus, Respondent, serving as Acting President, bears a specific 
constitutional duty to appoint those three individuals as Justices in order to fill the vacancies 
on the Court. Nevertheless, Respondent has, to this day, failed to appoint one of the three 
individuals elected by Claimant as a Justice. This constitutes a failure to discharge a specific 
constitutional duty to act. 
Respondent contends that, according to customary practice, consensus between the ruling 
and opposition parties is essential and that, because such consensus could not be confirmed, 
the appointment was withheld. However, there is no basis to recognize that a particular 
method of candidate recommendation as asserted by Respondent has become established as 
practice, nor can Claimant’s election procedure in this case be regarded as violating 
procedures grounded in the principle of parliamentary democracy.
D. It is impermissible for the President or the Acting President, on the ground that the 
appointment authority rests with him or her, to refuse without just cause to appoint a person 
5elected by Claimant, thereby hollowing out Claimant’s constitutionally granted power ––as 
the representative body of the people ––to form the Court. The failure to appoint at issue 
infringes Claimant’s constitutionally vested authority to form the Court through the election 
of Justices.
Summary of Concurring Opinion of Three Justices on Claim for Confirmation of 
Infringement of Authority 
I concur in the Court’s conclusion that the portion of the instant claim seeking confirmation 
of infringement of authority is justiciable and that the failure to appoint at issue constitutes an 
infringement of Claimant’s constitutional authority. However, I do not concur in the Court’s 
reasoning that a plenary session resolution is not required for the Speaker of the National 
Assembly to file a competence dispute claim on behalf of Claimant.
For a claim for confirmation of infringement of authority to be justiciable, a plenary 
session resolution is necessary. Therefore, a claim filed without satisfying such procedural 
requirement is nonjusticiable and cannot avoid dismissal. Nevertheless, because Claimant, 
during the pendency of this case, passed a resolution in a plenary session expressing its will 
to ratify this claim, the claim has become justiciable retroactively. 
Claimant, as the representative organ of the people, is composed of members elected as 
representatives of the entire people. Therefore, binding decisions of the National Assembly 
must be adopted in the plenary session, which consists of all elected members and in which 
all members participate. Since neither the Constitution nor relevant statutes prescribe 
procedures or requirements for decision-making when Claimant lodges a competence dispute 
claim against another State organ etc., the decision to file such a claim must follow Article 49 
of the Constitution and Article 109 of the National Assembly Act, which provide that the 
attendance of a majority of members in office and the concurring vote of a majority of those 
present are required for the actions of Claimant. Unless otherwise expressly provided by 
statute, no exception may be recognized. Even if an exception were to be allowed, it must be 
strictly limited to cases of necessity and only under clear and rigorous conditions. The 
reasons presented in the Court’s opinion do not amount to grounds that would justify such an 
exception to the constitutional rule. 
6* This translation is provisional and subject to revision.
